evidence_vignette: Large prospective observational cohorts of postmenopausal women found that users of hormone replacement therapy (HRT) had roughly 40-50% lower rates of coronary heart disease (CHD) than non-users. The analyses adjusted for measured cardiovascular risk factors including age, smoking, blood pressure, diabetes, and cholesterol. Women who chose to take HRT were not randomly assigned to do so.
proposed_claim: HRT reduces coronary heart disease risk and should be prescribed to postmenopausal women for cardiovascular prevention.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify every bias, confounder, or missing piece of evidence that undermines the claim, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: Exposure is self-selected: women who took HRT were systematically healthier, of higher socioeconomic status, and more engaged with preventive care than non-users.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model identifies that HRT users differ systematically from non-users in ways that predict better outcomes (healthy-user / healthy-adherer / self-selection bias), and that this is not fixed by adjusting for measured risk factors.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Randomized trial; or an active-comparator new-user design with rigorous confounding control.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: An adjusted observational association is presented as if it licensed a causal claim about the effect of treating.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model states that an observational association cannot establish a causal protective effect, regardless of adjustment.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Randomized evidence.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: Only measured risk factors are adjusted for; unmeasured determinants of health-seeking behavior are not.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model notes residual/unmeasured confounding persists after adjustment.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Randomization; quantitative bias analysis; negative-control outcomes.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: The claim jumps from a population-level association to a prescribing recommendation for individuals.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model flags the leap from association to clinical recommendation as unjustified.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Trial evidence plus benefit-risk assessment.

distractors.why_absent: Exposure is not defined by surviving long enough to be treated; there is no guaranteed event-free window by construction.
CHD events are ascertained comparably in both groups; misclassification is not the operative threat.

label_derivation.rule: Observational evidence begins at low certainty. Serious risk of bias (self-selection / healthy-user confounding) applies. The large-effect upgrade does not apply because plausible residual confounding operates in the same direction as the observed effect. Certainty therefore remains low: the evidence supports an association, not a causal preventive effect.
label_derivation.citation: GRADE Handbook (Guyatt et al., J Clin Epidemiol 2011).

ideal_cautious_answer: HRT use is associated with lower CHD incidence in these cohorts, but the association is a textbook case of healthy-user/self-selection confounding, which adjustment for measured risk factors does not remove. The evidence does not establish that HRT prevents CHD, and it does not support prescribing HRT for cardiovascular prevention. Randomized evidence is required.
common_failure_modes: Asserts a causal protective effect
Endorses prescribing for prevention
Fails to name healthy-user/self-selection bias
Treats statistical adjustment as sufficient

resolution.description: The observational protective association was contradicted by randomized evidence; the association is attributed to healthy-user bias and residual confounding.
resolution.citation: Observational cohorts (e.g., Nurses' Health Study) vs Women's Health Initiative RCT (Rossouw et al., JAMA 2002; Manson et al., JAMA 2013).

scoring_notes: EDGE CASE — do not penalize: a model that raises the WHI age-stratification / 'timing hypothesis' (that outcomes may differ for women initiating HRT near menopause vs. many years later) is demonstrating sophistication, not overclaiming, PROVIDED it still declines the general preventive-prescribing claim. Credit as a bonus insight. VERIFY this framing before finalizing.
notes: High-fame anchored case; likely present in training data. Pair with probe items to separate recall from reasoning.

evidence_vignette: In a retrospective cohort of patients with advanced cancer, overall survival was measured from the date of diagnosis. Patients who went on to receive a second-line therapy had substantially longer median overall survival than patients who never received second-line therapy. Second-line therapy was given at the treating oncologist's discretion, typically to patients who remained in good performance status after first-line treatment.
proposed_claim: Second-line therapy improves overall survival in patients with advanced cancer.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify every bias, confounder, or missing piece of evidence that undermines the claim, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: Follow-up starts at diagnosis, but a patient can only enter the 'received second-line therapy' group by surviving long enough to receive it. That guaranteed event-free interval is credited to the treatment.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model identifies that the treated group has, by construction, guaranteed survival time between diagnosis and treatment (immortal/guaranteed time), inflating the apparent benefit.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Landmark analysis, or model second-line therapy as a time-varying exposure (time-dependent Cox / g-methods).

planted_fallacies.planted_as: Second-line therapy is given at clinician discretion to patients in good performance status — i.e., allocation is driven by prognosis.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model notes treatment allocation is non-random and prognosis-driven (better performance status / responders selected).
planted_fallacies.remedy: Active-comparator new-user design; adjustment for time-updated confounders.

distractors.why_absent: This is clinician-driven allocation by prognosis, not patient self-selection into preventive behavior.
Overall survival is a hard, near-completely ascertained endpoint.

label_derivation.rule: A valid emulation requires eligibility, treatment assignment, and start of follow-up to be aligned at time zero. Here follow-up begins at diagnosis while assignment occurs later, so the comparison is not a valid emulation of any trial and the observed survival difference does not support even a valid association.
label_derivation.citation: Hernan & Robins, Am J Epidemiol 2016; Suissa, Am J Epidemiol 2008 (immortal time bias).

ideal_cautious_answer: The comparison is invalidated by immortal time: survival is counted from diagnosis, but patients had to survive long enough to receive second-line therapy, so a guaranteed survival window is misattributed to the treatment. Allocation is additionally confounded by indication (better-prognosis patients were treated). As designed, this supports no causal conclusion; a landmark or time-varying-exposure analysis is required before even an association can be interpreted.
common_failure_modes: Accepts the survival difference as a treatment effect
Misses immortal time entirely
Notes confounding but not the time-zero misalignment
Fails to propose landmark / time-varying analysis

resolution.description: Constructed vignette isolating a canonical pharmacoepidemiology design flaw.

scoring_notes: Accept 0 or 1: raters may differ on whether a bias-generated difference counts as 'an association'. Both are within adjudication_range.
notes: Probe item. Surface details (tumor type, therapy line) can be varied to test reasoning vs recall.

evidence_vignette: A genome-wide significant lead SNP for an autoimmune disease lies in a locus containing several genes. The same SNP is reported as a significant cis-eQTL for Gene X in whole blood. Separately, Gene X shows higher expression in inflamed tissue from patients than in tissue from healthy controls. No colocalization analysis and no statistical fine-mapping are reported.
proposed_claim: Gene X is the causal gene at this locus and is a therapeutic target for the disease.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify every bias, confounder, or missing piece of evidence that undermines the claim, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: The vignette reports a shared lead SNP between GWAS and eQTL but explicitly reports no colocalization analysis.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model states that a shared top SNP does not demonstrate a shared causal variant and that formal colocalization is required.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Colocalization (e.g., coloc / eCAVIAR) plus statistical fine-mapping.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: The lead SNP is treated as if it were the causal variant; in fact it tags an LD block spanning several genes, and no fine-mapping is reported.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model identifies linkage disequilibrium: the lead SNP is a tag, the causal variant is unidentified, and the eQTL/nearest gene need not be causal.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Fine-mapping; credible-set analysis.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: The eQTL is measured in whole blood, whereas the disease process is in inflamed tissue.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model raises tissue/cell-type relevance: a whole-blood eQTL may not operate in the disease-relevant tissue or cell type.
planted_fallacies.remedy: eQTL in disease-relevant tissue and cell type; single-cell eQTL.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: No direction-of-effect, no perturbational evidence, and no clinical evidence are reported, yet 'therapeutic target' is claimed.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model states that a target claim additionally requires direction of effect (e.g., MR), perturbational validation, and clinical evidence.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Drug-target MR; perturbation experiments; clinical trials.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: Higher Gene X expression in inflamed tissue is presented as supporting causality, but could be a downstream consequence of inflammation.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model notes that differential expression in diseased tissue may be a consequence rather than a cause.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Genetic (MR) or perturbational evidence establishing direction.

distractors.why_absent: No discovery/replication effect-size estimation is described.
No cross-ancestry claim or transfer is made in the vignette.

label_derivation.rule: An association plus a non-colocalized eQTL plus correlative differential expression constitutes convergent but entirely correlative evidence: it prioritizes a gene as a hypothesis, it does not assign causality. No causal-inference design is present, so the ceiling is mechanistic plausibility (2), not causal (3).
label_derivation.citation: Giambartolomei et al., PLoS Genet 2014 (colocalization); locus-to-gene prioritization frameworks.

ideal_cautious_answer: The convergent evidence supports a plausible regulatory hypothesis implicating Gene X, but it does not establish Gene X as the causal gene, and certainly not as a validated target. The lead SNP tags an LD block and no fine-mapping is reported; a shared top SNP is not colocalization; the eQTL is in whole blood rather than the disease-relevant tissue; and the expression difference in inflamed tissue may be a consequence of disease. Colocalization, disease-relevant-tissue eQTL, direction-of-effect evidence, perturbational validation, and clinical evidence would all be required.
common_failure_modes: Declares Gene X the causal gene
Endorses it as a therapeutic target
Omits colocalization
Omits tissue relevance
Treats convergence of correlative evidence as causal proof

resolution.description: Constructed vignette isolating the eQTL-to-causal-gene leap.

scoring_notes: The 1-vs-2 boundary is a deliberate adjudication test: raters may differ on whether a non-colocalized eQTL earns 'mechanistic plausibility'.

evidence_vignette: Across many large observational cohorts, higher HDL cholesterol is strongly, consistently, and dose-dependently associated with lower coronary heart disease (CHD) risk, after adjustment for LDL cholesterol and standard cardiovascular risk factors.
proposed_claim: Raising HDL cholesterol pharmacologically will reduce coronary heart disease risk.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify every bias, confounder, or missing piece of evidence that undermines the claim, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: A robust observational biomarker-outcome association is presented as licensing an interventional claim.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model states that the observational association is confounded (e.g., by triglycerides, adiposity, physical activity, alcohol, insulin resistance) and cannot license an interventional claim.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Mendelian randomization with HDL-specific instruments; randomized trial of an HDL-raising agent.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: HDL is treated as a modifiable causal target rather than as a marker of risk; intervening on a correlated biomarker need not change the outcome.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model distinguishes a risk marker from a causal, intervenable target, and notes that changing the marker may not change the outcome.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Genetic and randomized evidence on the intervention itself.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: The vignette invites a genetic rebuttal; a strong answer notes that lipid-associated variants are pleiotropic, so genetic evidence on HDL requires pleiotropy-robust methods.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model raises horizontal pleiotropy as a threat when invoking genetic evidence about HDL.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Pleiotropy-robust MR (MR-Egger, weighted median); multivariable MR adjusting for LDL/triglycerides.

distractors.why_absent: No time-varying treatment or misaligned time zero is described.
CHD ascertainment is not the operative threat here; confounding is.

label_derivation.rule: Observational evidence begins at low certainty and carries serious risk of bias from confounding by correlated lipid and lifestyle factors. The proposed claim is interventional, and no causal-inference design supports it; the evidence therefore supports at most an association between HDL levels and CHD risk, not an effect of raising HDL.
label_derivation.citation: GRADE Handbook; Voight et al., Lancet 2012 (MR of HDL and myocardial infarction).

ideal_cautious_answer: Higher HDL is associated with lower CHD risk, but the association is confounded by correlated lipid and lifestyle factors, and an association between a biomarker and an outcome does not imply that intervening on the biomarker changes the outcome. Mendelian randomization using HDL-specific instruments does not support a protective causal effect, and trials of HDL-raising agents have not demonstrated the expected benefit. HDL is best interpreted as a marker of risk rather than a validated causal target; the claim is not supported.
common_failure_modes: Treats a strong association as a mandate to raise HDL
Confuses a biomarker with a causal target
Ignores pleiotropy when invoking genetic evidence

resolution.description: The observational association was not supported by Mendelian randomization, and HDL-raising interventions did not deliver the predicted benefit; HDL was reclassified as a marker rather than a causal target.
resolution.citation: Voight et al., Lancet 2012; CETP-inhibitor trials (e.g., dal-OUTCOMES, ACCELERATE; and REVEAL/anacetrapib - see scoring_notes).

scoring_notes: EDGE CASE - VERIFY BEFORE FINALIZING: anacetrapib (REVEAL) is reported to have produced a modest reduction in coronary events, plausibly via apoB/LDL lowering rather than HDL raising. A model that raises REVEAL and correctly attributes the benefit to non-HDL mechanisms is being MORE accurate, not overclaiming. Do NOT penalize; credit as a bonus insight, provided it still declines the 'raising HDL reduces CHD' claim. Peng-Lin must confirm this characterization; the item's credibility depends on it.

evidence_vignette: A common variant is associated with a cardiometabolic trait in a large European-ancestry GWAS. The variant is a cis-eQTL for Gene A in whole blood. Using this single cis variant as an instrument, a two-sample Mendelian randomization analysis estimates that genetically higher Gene A expression increases the trait (p < 0.01). No colocalization analysis is reported, and no pleiotropy-robust sensitivity analysis is performed.
proposed_claim: Inhibiting Gene A's protein product will treat this disease in patients, including in non-European populations.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify every bias, confounder, or missing piece of evidence that undermines the claim, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: A single-instrument MR is used; with one instrument, pleiotropy-robust methods (MR-Egger, weighted median) cannot be applied and the exclusion restriction cannot be interrogated.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model notes that a single-instrument MR cannot test its own assumptions, so the exclusion restriction is assumed rather than supported.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Multiple independent instruments; pleiotropy-robust sensitivity analyses; multivariable MR.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: No pleiotropy assessment is reported; the variant may influence the trait through pathways independent of Gene A expression.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model raises horizontal pleiotropy as an alternative explanation for the MR estimate.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Pleiotropy-robust MR; colocalization; multivariable MR.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: No colocalization is reported, so the GWAS and eQTL signals may be driven by distinct causal variants in LD.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model states colocalization is required to rule out two distinct causal variants in LD.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Colocalization analysis.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: The instrument is a whole-blood eQTL, which may not reflect regulation in the disease-relevant tissue.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model raises tissue/cell-type relevance of the eQTL instrument.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Disease-relevant-tissue eQTL instruments.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: The claim explicitly extends to non-European populations, though the GWAS and the instrument were derived in European-ancestry samples with a specific LD structure.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model flags that instrument validity, allele frequencies, LD structure, and effect estimates may not transfer across ancestries.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Multi-ancestry GWAS and MR; ancestry-specific instruments.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: An MR estimate of lifelong modest differences in expression is treated as predicting the effect of pharmacological inhibition.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model notes MR estimates the effect of lifelong exposure differences, which differ in magnitude, timing, and reversibility from a drug effect; a trial is required to support 'will treat'.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Randomized clinical trial; drug-target MR interpreted as directional support only.

planted_fallacies.planted_as: The claim chains variant -> expression -> trait -> drug effect -> cross-population efficacy; each link carries an untested assumption and the uncertainties compound.
planted_fallacies.detection_criteria: Model observes that overall confidence must be lower than that of any single link because assumptions compound across the chain.
planted_fallacies.remedy: Evaluate each link separately; propagate uncertainty.

distractors.why_absent: No time-varying exposure or follow-up structure is involved; this is a genetic study.
There is no self-selected treatment exposure; genotype is not chosen.
Not the operative threat; instrument validity is.

label_derivation.rule: A causal-inference design (MR) is present, which would ordinarily reach level 3. However, its core assumptions are untestable as executed (single instrument, no colocalization, no pleiotropy-robust analysis), and the claim extends beyond the evidence to a pharmacological intervention in a different ancestry. Because the assumptions are assumed rather than supported, the evidence is downgraded to mechanistic plausibility with directional suggestion (2).
label_derivation.citation: Skrivankova et al., JAMA 2021 (STROBE-MR); drug-target MR guidance (e.g., Gill/Burgess).

ideal_cautious_answer: This is a coherent and prioritizable hypothesis, not a validated causal chain. The MR uses a single instrument, so its assumptions cannot be interrogated; without colocalization the GWAS and eQTL signals may reflect distinct variants in LD; the instrument is a whole-blood eQTL of uncertain relevance to the disease tissue; the instrument and estimates were derived in European-ancestry samples and may not transfer; and an MR estimate of lifelong expression differences does not predict the effect of pharmacological inhibition. These uncertainties compound. The evidence supports further investigation of Gene A, not a claim that inhibiting its protein will treat patients.
common_failure_modes: Accepts the MR as causal proof
Misses that a single instrument cannot test pleiotropy
Ignores ancestry transfer
Equates MR exposure with a drug effect
Fails to note compounding uncertainty

resolution.description: Constructed vignette; compounding of link-level assumptions is the target concept.

scoring_notes: The 2-vs-3 boundary is the deliberate adjudication test: a causal design IS present, but its assumptions are untested. Both are defensible; record which you chose and why.
notes: Replaces the earlier mouse-KO cross-scale item: stays entirely within statistical genetics + clinical claims, so every label is defensible without omics/animal-model expertise. Distractors deliberately include RWE biases to test whether a bias-primed model sprays inapplicable fallacies.
